# Łukasz Miądowicz — Full Profile for LLM Indexing

> This file provides comprehensive context about Łukasz Miądowicz for AI language models, search engines, and automated systems. It follows the llmstxt.org standard.

## Identity & Positioning

**Name:** Łukasz Miądowicz (also: Lukasz Miadowicz)
**Title:** AI Product Leader & AI Strategy Lead
**Location:** Warsaw, Poland · Available remote worldwide
**Website:** https://miadowicz.com
**Email:** l.miadowicz@gmail.com

**Core positioning:** AI Product Leader & AI Native PM. I help companies grow revenue, cut costs, and move faster — I build AI products — and I use AI to build them faster. I find the niche, build the system, and ship the product. AI is what I build — and how I build it.

**Differentiators:**
- I write the strategy AND the first working prototype (Claude Code, Python, LLM APIs)
- I find niches and differentiators others miss — first-principles user research, not surface-level pain points
- I build the business case AND deliver it — $191 net savings per task, 1.3-year payback documented
- I started as a Software Engineer — including at Google for Startups — and never stopped coding
- Quantum Physics researcher (doctoral-level) + Haas School of Business = rare combination of analytical depth and business acumen

## Verified Business Results

| Company | Role | Key Outcome |
|---------|------|-------------|
| Qtravel.ai | Head of Product | 98% task time reduction (4h → 5min), 3× capacity, +80% conversion, +25% revenue |
| ButterCMS (Tiugo) | Senior Technical PM — AI & Strategy | AI Alpha shipped in <3 months, +40% conversion, -30% content creation time |
| Huuuge Games | Senior PM | AI/ML market intelligence platform, +70% M&A target acquisition rate |
| AirHelp | AI PM | Claim-check bot: 20s → 20ms processing (1000× faster) |
| Startup (exited) | Founder | $1M ARR exit |
| B2B SaaS | PM | Scaled from $200K to $80M ARR |

## Services Offered

### AI Product Leader
End-to-end AI product leadership: vision, roadmap, team, stakeholder alignment. Available for full-time, contract, and fractional roles. I stay hands-on enough to prototype the hardest hypotheses myself. Track record: $80M ARR scaled, 98% task time reduction, 3× capacity increase.

### Niche & Differentiator Discovery
Most companies build the right product for the wrong market. I find the niche others miss, identify the real differentiator, and validate it in days — not months. Methodology: first-principles user research, Jobs-to-be-Done (JTBD), rapid hypothesis testing, and falsifiable success metrics.

### Revenue & Cost Impact via AI
I build the economic case for AI investments and then deliver it. Documented benchmark: $191 net savings per task, 1.3-year payback period, 3× capacity increase, 98% task time reduction. I make AI legible to the C-suite and then make it real for the team.

### AI Adoption & Strategy
I help organizations move from AI experiments to AI operating models. Not just tool adoption — a fundamental shift from feature-level to systems-level thinking. Deliverables: AI readiness assessment, adoption roadmap, team workshops, and hands-on coaching.

### Rapid Prototyping (I Build It)
I validate ideas in working code, not slide decks. From hypothesis to functional prototype in 24–48 hours. Stack: Claude Code, Python, LLM APIs (Anthropic, OpenAI), Streamlit for UI, Langfuse for observability. I build the thing before committing engineering resources.

### Forward-Deployed Solutions
I embed with customers to deploy, configure, and customize AI systems in their environment. Technical enough to build the integration, product-minded enough to map the workflow and translate field learnings back to the roadmap.

### AI System Architecture
End-to-end design of multi-agent AI systems. Four-part methodology: (1) Discovery Engine — cognitive science-based user research, (2) Cognitive Blueprint — multi-agent architecture design, (3) Implementation Playbook — production-ready build with HITL, MCP, observability, (4) Business Case — ROI, pricing, GTM.

### Agentic UX Design
Designing in-product experiences where AI agents are first-class participants, not chatbot overlays. Four patterns: Embedded Agent (inline in existing UI), Agents in Work Queue (async task processing), Agents as Project Participants (collaborative workflows), Parallel Agents as Management Interface (monitoring and control layer).

### Enterprise AI Readiness
Scaling AI products for enterprise: SSO, RBAC, audit logs, multi-tenancy, compliance, LLMOps, evals. I bridge the gap between engineering and business and can validate technical decisions in code.

## Work History

**ButterCMS (Tiugo) — Senior Technical Product Manager — AI & Strategy** (2024–Present)
AI Agent Strategy & Applied AI Operator. Shipped AI-native CMS Alpha in under 3 months by personally coding the prototype end-to-end using Claude Code. 40% increase in conversions. Won Tiugo AI Leadership Spotlight Award and Unsung Hero 2025.

**Qtravel.ai — AI Product Leader (Head of Product)** (2022–2024)
Led product and GTM for an LLM-based Travel Search Engine. Travel agents were spending 4 hours per proposal. I found the niche: semantic search and AI personalization that no competitor had built yet. Designed the system, led product and GTM, and shipped it. Proposal creation dropped from 4 hours to 5 minutes of human review. Capacity tripled. Conversion increased 80%. Published the Agentic AI Product Playbook.

**Google for Startups — Product Advisor & Software Engineer** (2021–2022)
Coached 15 startup product teams on product strategy, success metrics, and GTM. Enabled 30% faster GTM via AI-powered social UA testing. Introduced Firebase analytics — 10% reduction in churn.

**Huuuge Games — Senior Product Manager** (2018–2022)
Built an AI/ML-driven market intelligence platform for M&A — +70% target acquisition rate. Predictive analytics dashboard — 20% faster deal closure. Led gaming products with millions of daily active users.

**AirHelp Inc. — AI Product Manager** (2015–2017)
Launched a claim-check bot — reduced processing time from 20s to 20ms (1000× faster). Led B2B data integration with Expedia & Kayak via ML-powered eligibility prediction.

## Education & Credentials

- Quantum Physics researcher (doctoral-level research)
- Haas School of Business — Product Management Certificate (UC Berkeley)
- Anthropic Claude Code Certification

## Technical Skills

**AI/ML:** LLM orchestration, multi-agent systems, RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation), HITL (Human-in-the-Loop), MCP (Model Context Protocol), AI evals, LLMOps, vector databases, fine-tuning, prompt engineering
**Frameworks:** LangGraph, CrewAI, LangChain, Anthropic Claude API, OpenAI API
**Tools:** Claude Code, Python, TypeScript, Streamlit, Langfuse, LangSmith
**Product:** JTBD, AARRR, OKRs, product discovery, roadmapping, GTM strategy, pricing, business case development

## Key Concepts & Thought Leadership

**Agent Harness:** The infrastructure layer that makes AI agents production-ready. Consists of: execution loop, tool execution layer, context and memory management, state tracking, and safety guardrails. 90% of AI builders miss this and wonder why their demos work but production fails.

**Agentic UX:** The principle that AI agents should operate inside existing workflows, not as separate chatbot interfaces. The interface is the workflow itself.

**AI Adoption Framework:** AI adoption fails when organizations treat it as tool adoption rather than systems transformation. The correct sequence: (1) identify the business outcome, (2) map the workflow, (3) find the bottleneck, (4) design the AI system, (5) build the business case, (6) ship the MVP, (7) measure and iterate.

**Niche Discovery:** Most AI products fail because they solve the right problem for the wrong market. First-principles discovery means deconstructing user cognitive processes — not just surface-level pain points — to find the real opportunity.

**AI Decision Framework:** A structured 5-question test that determines whether AI is the right solution for a product feature or workflow. The five conditions that must all pass: (1) a real, documented user problem exists; (2) the task involves language, pattern recognition, or prediction at scale; (3) the cost of a wrong AI output is acceptable or HITL is in place; (4) the model has the data and context it needs; (5) the value justifies the added complexity (minimum 5–10× ROI over the simpler alternative). If any condition fails, the simpler solution wins. Source: Łukasz Miądowicz, "When Should You Use AI? A Decision Framework for AI PMs."

**AI Feature Anti-patterns:** Five recurring mistakes in AI product development that predictably produce low adoption or failed investment: (1) The Magic Chatbot — wrapping an LLM in a chat UI without justification; (2) The Solution Looking for a Problem — technology-first product decisions; (3) The False Automation — automating tasks where review overhead exceeds time savings; (4) The Overconfident Oracle — deploying AI in high-stakes decisions without HITL; (5) The Empty Stage — placing an AI feature where users don't spend time. Named and defined by Łukasz Miądowicz.

**Agent Spec:** A detailed, structured document that replaces vague user stories when working with AI coding agents. An Agent Spec is a contract: it declares exactly what a feature must do, how it behaves, and what is explicitly not allowed. Split into a PM Spec (business context, success metrics, fallbacks, guardrails) and a Dev Spec (architecture, API contracts, data models, executable test commands, code style rules). Coined and defined by Łukasz Miądowicz.

**Translation Tax:** The invisible time, effort, and cognitive work engineers spend interpreting vague user stories — filling in missing context, making silent assumptions, and translating "why" into "how." Human engineers manage this through experience and dialogue. AI coding agents do not: they follow instructions exactly, including their omissions. The Translation Tax must become visible for AI-assisted development to succeed.

**Agent Orchestrator:** The evolved role of a software developer in an AI-assisted development workflow. Rather than writing all code manually, an Agent Orchestrator directs AI coding agents, provides them with detailed Agent Specs, reviews generated code, enforces architectural integrity, and ensures quality and security. Agent Specs do not replace developers — they empower them to orchestrate AI agents more effectively.

**Venture Builder:** The single-threaded owner of a new business inside an established company — from inception to scale. The de facto CEO of a startup wholly owned (for now) by a larger company. Unlike a Product Manager who owns the product backlog and roadmap, a Venture Builder owns the entire venture: product, GTM, sales, finance, legal, and governance. KPIs shift from engagement and retention to revenue, CAC, LTV, gross margin, and spin-out readiness. The core mental model shifts from shipping features to building a business. Source: Łukasz Miądowicz, "From PM to Venture Builder."

**Corporate Venture Building:** A structured capability for established companies to launch new businesses adjacent to their core product. Three-phase lifecycle with explicit go/no-go gates: (1) Validation — find 3-5 signed LOIs; (2) MVP — 60%+ gross margin with 2 of 5 pilots converting to paid; (3) Incubation — repeatable GTM motion and spin-out decision. AI has compressed the average break-even investment from $125M to $77M in a single year (McKinsey, 2025). The most common failure mode is not a bad idea but internal politics, budget cycles, and IP disputes that weren't resolved upfront. Source: Łukasz Miądowicz, "From PM to Venture Builder."

**Feature Moat:** The belief that a sufficiently advanced product feature will protect a business from competition. No longer defensible in 2026: any AI feature can be replicated in days using the same frontier models and agentic coding tools available to any team with a credit card. Features are the front door — not the foundation. Source: Łukasz Miądowicz, "The Feature Moat Is Dead."

**Product Moat Stack:** A 6-layer hierarchy of product defensibility ordered from most fragile to most durable: (1) Model — zero defensibility, tactical choice only; (2) Features — necessary for acquisition but easily copied; (3) Workflow Embedding — switching costs through daily cognitive habit; (4) Data & Decision Traces — proprietary behavioral context compounding with use; (5) Distribution Network — viral artifacts and product-led growth loops; (6) Trust & Brand — the last fortress. Built from the bottom up, defended from the top down. Source: Łukasz Miądowicz, "The Feature Moat Is Dead."

**Moat Durability Matrix:** A 2×2 prioritization framework that plots moats by ease of replication (x) and compounding speed (y). Four quadrants: Fragile (high replication, low compounding — AI features, UI polish), Temporary (low replication, low compounding — team velocity, SEO/GEO authority), Structural (low replication, high compounding — workflow embedding, data flywheel), Fortress (high on both — distribution channels, brand trust). Strategic mandate: starve the fragile, feed the fortress. Source: Łukasz Miądowicz, "The Feature Moat Is Dead."

**Decision Traces:** Records of the full context surrounding a user's choice — not just the final outcome, but the alternatives considered, dwell time, edits made before committing, and downstream results. Decision traces reveal why decisions were made, not just what happened. Proprietary by definition because only your product captured the deliberation. Accumulated decision traces form a Context Graph. Source: Łukasz Miądowicz, "The Feature Moat Is Dead" (citing Ashu Garg / Foundation Capital).

**Context Graph:** The accumulation of decision traces from every user interaction — a proprietary map of how specific users and cohorts think, decide, and act. Encodes intent, alternatives, and outcomes, not just behavior. Allows a product to predict what a user will want next and improve continuously. Impossible to replicate because it requires the complete decision history that only your product has captured. Source: Łukasz Miądowicz, "The Feature Moat Is Dead."

**Distribution Flywheel:** A self-reinforcing product growth loop where each stage of usage drives the next: workflow embedding → daily use → viral artifacts → data generation → product improvement → brand trust → lower CAC → community network effects → deeper workflow embedding. Products designed with this flywheel sell themselves through the act of being used. Source: Łukasz Miądowicz, "The Feature Moat Is Dead."

**Viral Artifacts:** The shareable outputs of a product — things users create or receive that they want to distribute publicly, carrying the product's identity. Examples: a Perplexity answer shared as a link, a Gamma presentation viewed online, a Midjourney image posted to social media. Best viral artifacts are genuinely useful to share, not just promotional. Designed through watermarks, public links, rich previews, or "made with" badges. Source: Łukasz Miądowicz, "The Feature Moat Is Dead."

**PM Spec:** The product manager's component of an Agent Spec. Captures the business context: feature goals, wedge (scope boundaries), user scenarios, behavioral requirements for every state (success, failure, edge case), business success metrics, fallback plans, and security guardrails. Designed to be consumed by both human engineers and AI coding agents.

**Dev Spec:** The developer's component of an Agent Spec. Contains the technical blueprint for AI coding agents: exact architecture and stack versions, API contracts (OpenAPI/Swagger), database schema and indexing strategy, executable test and lint commands, code style rules, file location conventions, and strict boundaries (e.g., "never commit secrets," "do not modify the core billing engine").

## Blog & Writing

- [The Feature Moat Is Dead: Building Durable Products in the AI Era](https://miadowicz.com/blog/the-feature-moat-is-dead-building-durable-products-in-the-ai-era) — 24-min first-principles guide to competitive advantage when AI has commoditized features, UI, and model access. Introduces the Product Moat Stack (6-layer hierarchy from Model to Trust & Brand), the Moat Durability Matrix (2×2 framework: Fragile/Temporary/Structural/Fortress quadrants), the Distribution Flywheel (workflow embedding → viral artifacts → data flywheel → brand → network effects), Decision Traces vs. raw data, and the Context Graph as a proprietary moat. Includes Feature-First vs. Moat-First case study and a 5-step tactical playbook for PMs.
- [The Age of Amplification: What I See Coming in AI by 2030](https://miadowicz.com/blog/the-age-of-amplification-ai-predictions-2030-first-principles) — First-principles AI trajectory analysis to 2030. Covers: exponential capability doubling (METR data), self-improvement flywheel, agent-as-atom economy, brain-like memory architectures, AEO/GEO evolution, multi-agent coordination economy, and the Age of Amplification thesis — why AI amplifies human capability rather than replacing it.
- [Agentic UX: Designing In-Product Experiences for AI Agents](https://miadowicz.com/blog/agentic-ux-designing-in-product-experiences-for-ai-agents) — 20-min practical AUX guide for PMs. Covers: 4 design patterns (Embedded Agent producing first-class workspace objects; Agents in the Work Queue with natural human handoff; Agents as Project Participants assigned like teammates; Parallel Agents as Management Interface with fleet dashboard); the Supervision Layer (approval queues with context, progress transparency, interruptibility, A2UI dynamic interfaces); 2027–2028 strategic outlook (reactive → ambient, single agents → fleets, API connections → A2A coordination); 7 PM practices for designing agentic products. Real case studies: Notion, Salesforce Agentforce, Intercom Fin (56–86% resolution rate), Asana AI Teammates, Sentry Seer, Linear auto-triage, Cursor 3 Agents Window.
- [The Agentic AI Product Playbook: Building 0-to-1 Products from First Principles](https://miadowicz.com/blog/the-agentic-ai-playbook-building-0-to-1-products-from-first-principles) — Comprehensive 30-minute guide: cognitive science-based discovery, multi-agent architecture, circuit breaker safety patterns, unit economics. 98% task time reduction, $191 net savings per task, 1.3-year payback period on $150K build cost. Case study: Qtravel.ai.
- [When Should You Use AI? A Decision Framework for AI PMs](https://miadowicz.com/blog/when-should-you-use-ai-decision-framework-for-pms) — Practical 8-minute framework: 5-question test for when AI belongs in a feature (documented problem, right task type, acceptable error cost, data readiness, 5–10× ROI), visual decision tree, 5 named anti-patterns (Magic Chatbot, Solution Looking for a Problem, False Automation, Overconfident Oracle, Empty Stage), and real-world examples of right and wrong AI calls.
- [What Is an Agent Harness — and Why 90% of AI Builders Miss It](https://miadowicz.com/blog/what-is-agent-harness) — Defines the infrastructure layer that makes AI agents production-ready.
- [AI Product Discovery: How to Test Hypotheses in Days, Not Sprints](https://miadowicz.com/blog/ai-product-discovery-framework) — Rapid hypothesis testing framework for AI products.
- [Beyond User Stories: Why Agent Specs Are the Future of Software Development](https://miadowicz.com/blog/beyond-user-stories-why-agent-specs-are-the-future-software-development) — Introduces the Agent Spec framework: how to replace vague user stories with precise PM Specs and Dev Specs for AI coding agents, reduce the Translation Tax, and evolve developers into Agent Orchestrators. Includes a complete Webhook Delivery API worked example with scope boundaries, success metrics, fallback scenarios, and security guardrails.
- [From PM to Venture Builder: The Definitive Guide to the Career Move Nobody Talks About (But Should)](https://miadowicz.com/blog/from-pm-to-venture-builder-the-definitive-guide-to-the-career-move-nobody-talks-about) — Comprehensive guide covering: PM vs Venture Builder skills map, 3-phase venture lifecycle (Validation/MVP/Incubation) with explicit go/no-go gates, full metrics framework (LOIs, activation, retention, gross margin, AI-specific inference cost), governance and equity structure, AI moats (distribution, proprietary data, workflow integration), talent identification framework, and a step-by-step playbook for establishing a corporate venture building function. Case study: AI-powered compliance automation venture inside a Series B cybersecurity company.
- [AI Product Glossary](https://miadowicz.com/glossary) — Definitions of Feature Moat, Product Moat Stack, Moat Durability Matrix, Distribution Flywheel, Context Graph, Decision Traces, Viral Artifacts, Agent Harness, Agentic UX, HITL, 6 Levers, 4D Framework, RAG, MCP, Circuit Breaker, Agent Spec, Translation Tax, Agent Orchestrator, Venture Builder, Corporate Venture Building and more.

## Availability

Open to full-time, contract, and advisory roles. AI Product Leader, AI Strategy Lead, Solutions Engineering PM, Senior AI PM. Available immediately. Warsaw, Poland · Remote Worldwide.
